steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers conclude that net CO₂ is likely to increase when snow arrives early (before soil freezes). A supporting finding must explain the causal mechanism: why early snow would cause more CO₂ output than uptake during that window.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘The effect of soil temperature on the growth of vegetation and on the rate of heterotrophic respiration is stronger during the period of seasonal snow cover than during the period without snow cover.’: This says snow cover strengthens the soil-temperature effect on both vegetation growth and heterotrophic respiration, but it doesn't show why respiration would outpace plant uptake to produce a net CO₂ increase. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Relatively early snow cover reduces the amount of soil moisture available for the growth of plant species such as *R. arcticus* and lowers the rate of heterotrophic respiration.’: This reduces both processes, giving no clear reason why net CO₂ would increase — if respiration is also reduced, the balance is unclear. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Seasonal snow cover tends to persist longer in areas of relatively low vegetation growth and high heterotrophic respiration than in areas of relatively high vegetation growth and low heterotrophic respiration.’: This describes a geographic pattern, not a temporal mechanism explaining why early snow causes net CO₂ increase. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘The soil insulation provided by snow cover enables heterotrophic respiration to continue during a period in which plant species such as *R. arcticus* are typically not growing.’: This directly explains the mechanism: early snow insulates the soil so microorganisms keep releasing CO₂, but plants aren't yet active to absorb it — CO₂ output goes uncompensated, producing a net increase. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
